15: hos estin eikon tou theou tou aoratou prototokos pases ktiseos (hos estin eikōn tou theou tou aoratou, prōtotokos pasēs ktiseōs): 'who is the image of the invisible God, the firstborn of all creation.' The Colossian hymn (Colossians 1:15-20) is one of the highest Christological statements in the NT. Eikon (image) denotes not a copy of an absent original but the precise visible representation of an invisible reality — Christ is the icon in whom the otherwise-invisible God is seen (cf. John 1:18; Hebrews 1:3: apaugasma + charakter). Prōtotokos pasēs ktiseōs: 'firstborn of all creation' — prōtotokos is not 'first created' (which would be prōtoktistos) but the one who holds the firstborn's status of priority and authority over all creation; Colossians 1:16-17 confirms he is the creator, not a creature.
19: hoti en auto eudokesen pan to pleroma katoikesai (hoti en autō eudokēsen pan to plērōma katoikēsai): 'for in him all the fullness was pleased to dwell.' Pleroma (fullness) in Gnostic usage (slightly later than Colossians) denotes the totality of divine attributes distributed among aeons. Paul uses the term to assert the counter-claim: the entire divine fullness (not a portion distributed among spiritual intermediaries) resides bodily (somatikos, Colossians 2:9) in Christ. The Colossian false teaching apparently proposed a hierarchy of spiritual powers mediating access to God; the hymn's response is that Christ contains the whole without remainder.
1: Apostolos Christou Iēsou dia thelēmatos theou (apostle of Christ Jesus through the will of God): the same formula as 1 Corinthians 1:1 and 2 Corinthians 1:1, grounding apostolic authority not in self-appointment but in divine commission. Timothy is co-sender (adelphos, brother) though Paul alone is the apostle; Timothy's inclusion signals a shared missionary enterprise and lends the letter communal rather than merely personal authority.
2: Colossae was located in the Lykos River valley in southwestern Phrygia (Asia Minor), about 160 km east of Ephesus, near Laodicea and Hierapolis (cf. Colossians 4:13-16). Tois hagiois kai pistois adelphois en Christō (to the holy and faithful brothers in Christ): two simultaneous qualifiers — hagiois (holy, set apart by divine calling) and pistois (faithful, trusting). En Christō functions as a locative of sphere: the community exists within the orbit of Christ's lordship.
3: Eucharistoumen tō theō patri tou Kyriou hēmōn Iēsou Christou pantote peri hymōn proseuchomenoi (we give thanks to God the Father of our Lord Jesus Christ, always praying for you): Paul's letter openings characteristically include a thanksgiving section (eucharistia) that establishes rapport and anticipates the letter's major themes. 'God the Father of our Lord Jesus Christ' frames the entire letter's theology: the Father-Son relationship is the ground of all Christological claims that follow.
4: Akousantes tēn pistin hymōn en Christō Iēsou kai tēn agapēn tēn eis pantas tous hagious (having heard of your faith in Christ Jesus and your love toward all the holy ones): the faith-love pair prepares the way for the third term, hope (Colossians 1:5), as the grounding reality. Pistis en Christō Iēsou (faith in Christ Jesus) — en can be instrumental (faith through union with Christ) rather than purely directional, signaling participatory rather than merely cognitive trust.
5: Dia tēn elpida tēn apokeimenēn hymin en tois ouranois (on account of the hope laid up for you in the heavens): apokeimenēn (laid up/stored up) is a commercial and legal term for goods held in reserve — the hope is a secured deposit. Logos tēs alētheias tou euangeliou (word of truth of the gospel) — a genitive chain: the gospel is defined as the word of truth, implicitly contrasting it with the competing 'philosophy' (Colossians 2:8) circulating in Colossae.
6: Karpophoroun kai auxanomenon (bearing fruit and growing): the gospel is personified as an active agent that produces and expands — the same verbs as Genesis 1:28 (be fruitful and multiply), suggesting Paul frames gospel spread as a new-creational fruitfulness. Tēn charin tou theou en alētheia (the grace of God in truth) — the Colossians received the gospel as what it truly is; the phrase anticipates the contrast with a gospel-substitute (Colossians 1:23: do not move from the hope of the gospel).
7: Epaphras is identified as the probable founder of the Colossian church — Paul has not met them personally (Colossians 2:1). His title — diakonos (minister/servant) and pistos (faithful) — are standard Pauline co-worker designations. Hyper hymōn diakonos tou Christou (minister of Christ on our behalf): the hyper (on behalf of) marks Epaphras as Paul's representative to the Lykos valley; his ministry is apostolically authorized.
8: Ho kai dēlōsas hēmin tēn hymōn agapēn en pneumati (who also disclosed to us your love in the Spirit): Epaphras serves as the information channel between Paul and the Colossians. Agapēn en pneumati (love in the Spirit) — en pneumati locates the love within the Spirit's generative activity; community bond is Spirit-sourced, not merely social affinity.
9: Epignōsis tou thelēmatos autou en pasē sophia kai synesei pneumatikē (knowledge of his will in all wisdom and spiritual understanding): epignōsis is the intensified form of gnōsis — full or exact knowledge. This may anticipate the false teachers' claim to superior gnōsis (Colossians 2:23). Sophia (wisdom) and synesis (understanding/insight) are both qualified by pneumatikē — Spirit-given, not philosophically derived.
10: Peripatēsai axiōs tou Kyriou eis pasan areskeian (to walk worthily of the Lord unto all pleasing): peripateo (walk) is Paul's standard term for ethical conduct — a Hebraism. Axiōs (worthily) sets the standard: conduct calibrated to the Lord's character. Karpophorountes kai auxanomenoi tē epignōsei tou theou (bearing fruit and growing in the knowledge of God) — the same fruit/growth vocabulary applied to the gospel (Colossians 1:6) is now applied to the believers themselves.
11: Dynamoumenoi en pasē dynamei kata to kratos tēs doxēs autou (being empowered with all power according to the might of his glory): the triple power vocabulary (dynamoumenoi/dynamei/kratos) emphasizes the divine source of endurance. Hypomonē kai makrothymia (endurance and patience): hypomonē is steadfastness under circumstances; makrothymia is patience with persons. Together they cover the full testing-range of community life.
12: Hikanōsanti hymas eis tēn merida tou klērou tōn hagiōn en tō phōti (who qualified you for the share of the inheritance of the holy ones in the light): hikanōsanti (qualified/made sufficient) — God is the agent; the believers contribute nothing to their own eligibility. Klērou (inheritance allotment) echoes the OT land-distribution vocabulary (LXX Josh 14-19), now applied eschatologically. Tōn hagiōn (of the holy ones) — probably the angelic assembly of heaven, whose inheritance believers now share by grace.
13: Hos erysato hēmas ek tēs exousias tou skotous kai metestēsen eis tēn basileian tou huiou tēs agapēs autou (who rescued us from the authority of darkness and transferred us into the kingdom of the Son of his love): metestēsen (transferred) is a technical Hellenistic administrative term for moving a population from one territory to another — deportation/resettlement vocabulary applied to the cosmic transfer of believers from one dominion to another.
14: En hō echomen tēn apolutrōsin, tēn aphesin tōn hamartiōn (in whom we have the redemption, the forgiveness of sins): apolutrōsis (redemption) in its classical sense involves payment of a lytron (ransom price) to release a prisoner or slave. The genitive appositive aphesin tōn hamartiōn (forgiveness of sins) defines the content of redemption, grounding commercial imagery in the relational reality of forgiven guilt. Ephesians 1:7 has an almost identical formula, primary evidence for the Colossians-Ephesians literary relationship.
16: En autō ektisthē ta panta (in him all things were created): three prepositions structure Colossians 1:16-17 — en autō (in him), di' autou (through him), eis auton (for/toward him). The Son is the sphere, means, and goal of creation. Thronoi, kyriotētes, archai, exousiai (thrones, dominions, rulers, authorities) — the full hierarchy of angelic/cosmic powers; the Son created them all and therefore stands above them all, directly countering any spirit-hierarchy teaching in Colossae.
17: Autos estin pro pantōn kai ta panta en autō synestēken (he is before all things and all things hold together in him): pro pantōn implies both temporal priority (pre-existence) and rank priority. Synestēken (hold together) — the perfect tense of synistaō: the present result of a completed act of cohering. The Son is the ontological ground of cosmic coherence; this coheres with the Wisdom tradition (Wis Colossians 1:7; Sir Colossians 43:26) while grounding cosmic cohesion in a personal agent.
18: Autos estin hē kephalē tou sōmatos, tēs ekklēsias (he is the head of the body, the church): in Romans and 1 Corinthians the body metaphor applies to the local congregation; here (and in Ephesians) the 'body' is the universal church whose head is the cosmic Christ. Prōtotokos ek tōn nekrōn (firstborn from the dead) — a second prōtotokos establishes two spheres of supremacy: original creation (Colossians 1:15) and new creation. Hina genētai en pasin autos prōteuōn (so that he might be in all things pre-eminent) — comprehensive supremacy is the telos of both firstborn-titles.
20: Kai di' autou apokatallaxai ta panta eis auton, eirēnopoiēsas dia tou haimatos tou staurou autou (and through him to reconcile all things to himself, making peace through the blood of his cross): apokatallaxai (to reconcile completely) — the apo-prefix on katallassō intensifies toward completeness. The scope 'all things' (ta panta) — both earthly and heavenly — suggests cosmic rather than merely personal reconciliation. Eirēnopoiēsas dia tou haimatos tou staurou — the cross is the historical instrument of cosmic peace, an offense to both Jewish messianic expectation and Roman honor culture.
21: Kai hymas pote ontas apēllotriōmenous kai echthrous tē dianoia en tois ergois tois ponērois (and you, once being alienated and enemies in your mind in evil works): apēllotriōmenous (alienated/estranged) — the perfect passive participle describes a completed structural separation; Gentile believers were outside the covenant. Echthrous tē dianoia (enemies in the mind) — enmity located in the cognitive-volitional center, not merely in outward behavior, is a characteristically Pauline anthropological claim.
22: Nyni de apokatēllaxen en tō sōmati tēs sarkos autou dia tou thanatou parastēsai hymas hagious kai amōmous kai anegklētous katenōpion autou (but now he has reconciled in the body of his flesh through death, to present you holy and blameless and unaccusable before him): en tō sōmati tēs sarkos autou (in the body of his flesh) — the redundancy emphasizes physical reality of incarnation and death against any docetic reading. The three presentation-adjectives (hagious/amōmous/anegklētous) combine cultic (amōmos = without blemish, LXX sacrificial vocabulary) and forensic (anegklētos = unaccusable) imagery.
23: Ei ge epimenetei tē pistei tethemeliōmenoi kai hedraioi kai mē metakinoumenoi apo tēs elpidos tou euangeliou (if indeed you remain in the faith, having been founded and stable and not moved from the hope of the gospel): tethemeliōmenoi (founded) — perfect passive: founding is complete and fixed. Mē metakinoumenoi (not being moved) — a navigational/architectural image of a building being shaken from its foundation. The Colossian teachers are implicitly the destabilizing force. The conditional ei ge is motivational, not expressing doubt about the outcome.
24: Antanaplērō ta hysterēmata tōn thlipseōn tou Christou en tē sarki mou hyper tou sōmatos autou, ho estin hē ekklēsia (I fill up what is lacking of the afflictions of Christ in my flesh for the sake of his body, which is the church): hysterēmata tōn thlipseōn tou Christou (what is lacking of the afflictions of Christ) does not imply that Christ's atonement is incomplete. Rather, the messianic woes — the eschatological tribulations that precede the end (Daniel 12:1; Mark 13:19-20) — have an appointed measure; Paul's apostolic sufferings fill that eschatological quota for the church's sake.
25: Kata tēn oikonomian tou theou tēn dotheisan moi eis hymas plērōsai ton logon tou theou (according to the stewardship of God given to me toward you, to fulfill/complete the word of God): oikonomia (stewardship/administration) — household-management vocabulary applied to Paul's divinely assigned role in the economy of salvation. Plērōsai ton logon tou theou (to complete the word of God) — bringing the word to its intended reach (the Gentiles), not fulfilling a prophecy.
26: To mystērion to apokekrymmenon apo tōn aiōnōn kai apo tōn geneōn, nyn de ephanerōthē tois hagiois autou (the mystery hidden from the ages and from the generations, but now revealed to his holy ones): mystērion in Paul consistently means a formerly hidden divine purpose now publicly disclosed — not an esoteric secret. Apo tōn aiōnōn kai apo tōn geneōn — two measures of hiddenness: cosmological (aiōnes) and historical (geneai). Nyn de (but now) — the eschatological now of disclosure.
27: Christos en hymin, hē elpis tēs doxēs (Christ in you, the hope of glory): the revealed content of the mystery. En hymin (in you/among you) — the indwelling of Christ in Gentile believers is the mystery's specific content: God's presence, once tied to temple and Israel, now inhabits Gentile bodies. Hē elpis tēs doxēs (the hope that is/consists of glory) — the genitive of content: the eschatological glory to be revealed at Christ's Parousia.
28: Panta anthrōpon teleion en Christō (every person mature in Christ): the triple panta anthrōpon (every person) is rhetorically emphatic — the gospel is universal, not esoteric. Teleion en Christō (mature/complete in Christ) — teleios is the goal of Christian formation; the false teachers may have promised a higher teleiotēs through their practices. Paul's response: genuine maturity is in Christ alone, not in supplemental spiritual programs.
29: Kopiō agōnizomenos kata tēn energeian autou tēn energoumenēn en emoi en dynamei (I labor, striving according to his working that works in me in power): kopiō (labor, toil) describes demanding physical and mental work; agōnizomenos (striving, contending) is athletic/military contest vocabulary. The paradox of apostolic ministry: the effort is real, yet the energy source is divine. Energeia energoumenē (working being-worked) — a cognate construction emphasizing that divine operation is the ground of all human apostolic labor.
